coredata.dc:title: Green synthesized carbon quantum dot as dual sensor for Fe(II) ions and rational design of catalyst for visible light mediated abatement of pollutants
coredata.dc:creator: Mathew, Sneha
Rose Chacko, Anu
Korah, Binila K.
Susan Punnose, Mamatha
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: Herein we report the fabrication of carbon quantum dots through a green, cost-effective, and one-pot method from Wrightia tinctoria leaves. The CQDs are effective as fluorescent and electrochemical sensors for Fe(II) ions, with a detection limit of 2.3 nM. In the presence of Fe(II), strongly fluorescent quenching of CQDs occurred, providing the basis of Fe(II) quantification. The versatile sensing ability of CQDs in various real samples indicates the significant potential of carbon-based materials in developing sensing platforms for accurate and fast assay of different analytes. The synthesized green carbon dots and Bi4O5Br2 were fabricated on ZrO2 nanoparticles using a simple approach to create a high-efficiency ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 photocatalyst composite to degrade contaminants. The fabricated CQD-based nanocomposite successfully degraded rhodamine B. The catalyst composition was optimized by varying Bi4O5Br2 content, and the ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 (15 %) showed enhanced degradation efficiency and followed pseudo-first-order kinetics. More than 95 % of degradation efficiency in a short period and enhanced stability promise the nanocomposite's future industrial applications. This offers a novel approach to developing green carbon dots as a sensor for metal ions and its modified composites as visible-light-induced photocatalysts for the effective degradation of contaminants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Fe(II)
Fluorescence
ZrO2
Bi4O5Br2
Photocatalyst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Wrightia tinctoria carbon dot
Structure and morphology of carbon dot
Detection of Fe(II) by CQD through fluorescence method
Electrochemical sensing of Fe(II) by W. Tinctoria carbon dot modified pt electrode
PEIS, photocurrent density, reproducibility, and stability analysis
Application of CQDs probe in real samples
Photodegradation of rhodamine B using ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2
Structure and morphology of ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 photocatalyst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Greatly acknowledges the financial assistance received from the University Grants Commission (UGC) in the form of Junior Research Fellowship (JRF).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) HRTEM, (c) SAED, and (d) particle size histogram of CQD.
(a) XRD, and (b) Raman spectrum of CQD.
XPS spectra of CQD (a) survey scan and high-resolution spectra of (b) C, (c) N, and (d) O.
IR spectrum of CQD.
(a) Absorption (b) excitation-emission, and (c) emission spectra at different wavelengths of CQD.
Influence of (a) pH, (b) ionic strength, (c) photo irradiation, and (d) storage time on PL intensity of CQD.
(a) selectivity study, (b) Fluorescence spectra of the CQDs adding to different concentrations of Fe(II), (c) plot of concentration vs F0/F, and (d) interference study.
(a) CV runs, (b) dependence of pH, (c) dependence of scan rate, and (d) square root of scan rate vs current on the sensing of Fe(II) ions by the modified electrode.
(a) Selectivity study, (b) DPV analysis, and (c) plot of concentration of Fe(II) against peak current.
(a) PEIS, (b) photocurrent, (c) reproducibility, and (d) stability studies of CQD/GCE.
Real sample analysis of Fe(II) ions by PL analysis (n = 3).
(a) XRD, (b) TEM, (c) HRTEM, and (d) SAED of photocatalysts.
XPS (a) survey scan and high-resolution scans of (b) Zr, (c) Bi, (d) Br, (e) O, and (f) C in ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 (15%) composite.
(a) IR, and (b) DRS spectra of various catalysts.
Absorption spectra of RhB over (a) CQD, (b) ZrO2, (c) ZrO2/CQD, and (d) ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 (15%).
(a) Comparison of degradation (%) and (b) –ln C/C0 vs time graph of unmodified and modified catalysts.
Scavenger study.
(a) CV runs, (b) PEIS, (c) photocurrent responses of as-fabricated catalysts, and (d) fluorescence spectra of the catalysts ZrO2, ZrO2/CQD, and ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 (15%).
(a) Recycling runs of the catalyst for RhB, and (b) HPLC analysis.
Tauc’s plot of (a) ZrO2, (b) Bi4O5Br2 and Mott-Schottky plots for (c) ZrO2, (d) Bi4O5Br2.
Schematic representation of the fabrication carbon dot and ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 catalyst.
Proposed mechanism of metal ion sensing by CQD.
Mechanism of photocatalysis of ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 catalyst.

head.ce:title: Green synthesized carbon quantum dot as dual sensor for Fe(II) ions and rational design of catalyst for visible light mediated abatement of pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam 686560, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam 686560, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein we report the fabrication of carbon quantum dots through a green, cost-effective, and one-pot method from Wrightia tinctoria leaves. The CQDs are effective as fluorescent and electrochemical sensors for Fe(II) ions, with a detection limit of 2.3 nM. In the presence of Fe(II), strongly fluorescent quenching of CQDs occurred, providing the basis of Fe(II) quantification. The versatile sensing ability of CQDs in various real samples indicates the significant potential of carbon-based materials in developing sensing platforms for accurate and fast assay of different analytes. The synthesized green carbon dots and Bi4O5Br2 were fabricated on ZrO2 nanoparticles using a simple approach to create a high-efficiency ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 photocatalyst composite to degrade contaminants. The fabricated CQD-based nanocomposite successfully degraded rhodamine B. The catalyst composition was optimized by varying Bi4O5Br2 content, and the ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 (15 %) showed enhanced degradation efficiency and followed pseudo-first-order kinetics. More than 95 % of degradation efficiency in a short period and enhanced stability promise the nanocomposite's future industrial applications. This offers a novel approach to developing green carbon dots as a sensor for metal ions and its modified composites as visible-light-induced photocatalysts for the effective degradation of contaminants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are spherical nanoparticles with a diameter of less than 10 nm that have captivated researchers' attention since their discovery in 2004 [1]. Fluorescent CQDs show considerable potential for applications in various sectors, including bioimaging, sensor, catalysis, and optoelectronic devices, because of their exceptional optical properties, high stability, variable surface chemistry, and good biocompatibility [2–4]. They are considered fantastic candidates among the nanocarbon family due to the exquisite properties identified recently [5]. CQDs are becoming a viable alternative to metal-based quantum dots due to their composition and biocompatibility [6]. In vitro experiments gradually replace harmful heavy metal-based semiconductor quantum dots. They are also catalytically active due to their greater transferability [7] and are being examined as prospective replacements for standard fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots, which have several drawbacks, including heavy metal toxicity, high cost, and potential health and environmental hazards [8].

To make fluorescent CQDs, scientists have used various methods, including laser ablation, hydrothermal treatment, chemical oxidation, sun irradiation, electrochemical oxidation, and microwave treatment [9,10]. Low-cost and green synthetic CQDs can become more competitive fluorescent nanomaterials than semiconductor quantum dots with significant toxicity. Because of its advantages of simplicity, rapidity, and low pollutant emissions, hydrothermal synthesis is regarded as one of the most used CQDs synthesis processes [11–13]. Several research groups have reported the green synthesis of CQDs from Nigella sativa [1], banana peels [14], Mangifera indica leaves [15], Aloe vera [16]. CQDs, in particular, was successfully used in the detection of metal ions due to their strong and tunable photoluminescence capabilities. In addition to their new features, the ease with which they may be synthesized from a variety of carbon sources, including organic and inorganic materials, has piqued the interest of material scientists all over the world [17,18]. CQDs are being used to identify hazardous items like as pharmaceuticals, metal ions, insecticides, and other chemicals.

Iron is a vital microelement and the most abundant transition metal in the human body. Because it is the third most abundant metal in enzymes, its biological functions are frequently thought to be an essential cofactor in enzymes [19]. Iron is involved in diverse biological activities, including enzymatic reactions, oxygen transport, electron transport, and DNA synthesis and repair. An abnormal quantity of iron ions can disrupt cellular metabolism, resulting in various illnesses. Anemia, heart failure, intellectual loss, tissue inflammation, and cancer are diseases caused by iron homeostasis abnormalities. Furthermore, it can react with hydrogen peroxide to create the hydroxyl radical, the most damaging form of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in cells [11,20,21]. Therefore, detecting Fe(II) ions in biological systems and the environment is great concern.

Toxic organic dyes and pesticides released into the environment pose a severe hazard to the ecosystem and other living things. For example, Rhodamine B is an organic dye commonly used in biochemical analysis, cosmetics, and paper manufacturing. The discharge of these dyes into the environment creates a severe hazard to humans and the environment [22,23]. So, the removal of these dyes by proper methods is a serious concern. Hence it is necessary to develop an efficient catalyst for the degradation of these pollutants. Because of their ease of manufacture, broad solar spectrum absorption, optical stability, and biocompatibility, CQDs can be an excellent candidate for photocatalytic activity [24]. CQDs have been used to modify photocatalyst composites to increase their photocatalytic activity because of their superb charge carrier transfer, up-converted PL (UPCL) effect, and photosensitization [25]. Zirconium oxide is classified as a semiconductor material [26]. Several photocatalysts based on Zr (ZrO2, ZrTiO4, ZrPO4) have been reported to have more excellent activity when exposed to visible light [26,27]. Additionally, due to their adequate band gap and significant visible-light absorption, Bi-based photocatalysts are promising candidates for photocatalysis applications [28,29]. As a result, this semiconductor could help improve visible-light absorption of ZrO2 following heterojunction formation.

Wrightia tinctoria (W. tinctoria) is a deciduous tree with smooth scaly bark and a light grey tint. It is endemic to Burma and India and belongs to the Apocynaceae family [30]. W. tinctoria leaf extract has a wide range of medical uses, particularly in treating E.coli and Staphylococcus aureus infections. The leaves of this plant show the presence of terpenes, several tri-terpenoids, and flavonoids [31]. Studies reported that leaf extract has hydrogen donation potential and antioxidant properties and are considered essential for the production of efficient intensive scavengers and nanoparticles [32].

Herein, we report the one-step hydrothermal synthesis of carbon quantum dots from W. tinctoria leaves for efficient fluorescent and electrochemical detection of Fe(II) ions for the first time. The current study provides a synthetic approach free of harmful ingredients and time-consuming post-chemical procedures such as ultrafiltration, dialysis, and centrifugation to prepare CQDs. Moreover, we have improved the photocatalytic efficiency of the CQD by forming a composite with ZrO2 and Bi4O5Br2( Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Wrightia tinctoria carbon dot
ce:section.ce:para: W.tinctoria leaves were collected from the nearby area, washed with distilled water, and chopped leaves and distilled water were taken in a 250 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, heated at 180 0C for 8 h (hydrothermal reaction). The reactor was allowed to cool at room temperature once the reaction was completed. The obtained dark brown solution was centrifuged (1000 rpm, 10 m), filtered, and stored at 4 0C for further use.

ZrO2/CQD was prepared by dispersing 0.5 g of ZrOCl2·8H2O and 0.5 mL of CQD in 100 mL H2O by ultrasonication for 15 min. The obtained product was dried at 80 0C for 24 h. 0.5 g of the prepared ZrO2/CQD was kept in ultrasonication for 30 min to disperse the powder in 100 mL of water completely. The suspension was then added to a solution containing 0.15 g bismuth nitrate (Bi (NO3)3·5H2O) and water (15 mL). 10 mL aqueous solution containing 0.08 g cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) was progressively poured into the aforementioned suspension after 2 h of stirring. The pH was adjusted to 8 by adding NaOH and stirring for 15 min. The solution was autoclaved at 200 0C for 10 h, centrifuged, washed, and dried after hydrothermal treatment. Different weight percentages of the catalysts were obtained using the above method.

In a typical procedure, a known volume and concentration of carbon dot and metal ion were mixed in a cuvette with pH 6 buffer solution, sonicated for 3 min, and a fluorescent spectrum was recorded. Sensitivity towards Fe(II) ions was analyzed with a concentration ranging from 0 to 50 μM. A similar procedure was followed to conduct the interference from other metal ions.

The bare GCE was polished with 0.05 M alumina slurry, and crude CQD solution was dispersed in Nafion solution, and the mixture was drop cast on the surface of the electrode and dried in air. The selectivity of the sensor was investigated using cyclic voltammetry by a three-electrode method. Differential pulse voltammetry was performed with various concentrations of Fe(II).

The practical utility of the CQD sensor was evaluated by utilizing it to determine Fe(II) levels in different water samples. For this, we have taken tap water and water from the Pampa river (Kerala). The samples were filtered using 0.22 m Millipore filter paper to remove undesired solid suspensions. The water samples were spiked with an adequate amount of 0.001 M Fe(II) ion standard solutions and fluorescence spectra were recorded.

The photocatalysis experiments were studied by elimination of rhodamine B under a visible-light source. In each test, 0.01 g of the photocatalyst was added to the pollutant, stirred, and kept dark for 15 min. After light exposure, photocatalysts were retrieved at specific intervals of time, and UV spectra were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology of carbon dot
ce:section.ce:para: TEM analysis was carried out to obtain the structure and morphology of the carbon dot. TEM image clearly shows the dispersed spherical particles (Fig. 1 a). The lattice spacing was 0.208 nm in the HRTEM, which is near the (002) facet of graphite (Fig. 1b) [32,33]. The broad circular ring in the SAED micrograph pattern indicates the amorphous nature of the CQDs (Fig. 1c). The size distribution histogram confirms that the particle size ranges from 1 to 7 nm with an average size of 3.61 nm (Fig. 1d). The size was confirmed with DLS experiments and found that the size varies between 1 and 10 nm ( Fig. S1 ).

XRD peak observed at 2θ value 21.010 corresponds to diffraction from (002) lattice plane of amorphous carbon (Fig. 2 a). The extent of structural defects in CQDs was determined using Raman analysis. Raman image showed two distinct peaks at 1347 and 1582 cm−1, corresponding to the disorder (D band) and graphite (G band) nanostructures, respectively (Fig. 2b). The ID/IG ratio is connected to the degree of graphitization. The ID/IG is calculated as 0.376, indicating highly graphitic [34,35].

The aqueous solution of CQDs is brown in daylight while it emits green fluorescence under UV light. The broadband around 273 nm in the UV–vis. spectrum associated with n–π* transitions of CO groups (Fig. 5 a). The excitation and emission spectra are shown in Fig. 5b. The varied emission wavelengths of CQDs obtained with different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 5c (330 to 400 nm), As the excitation wavelength increases, the emission wavelength is red-shifted. As a result of this feature, they can be used as fluorescent probes in a number of sensing applications. The maximum emission peak was obtained at 380 nm excitation wavelength and decreased with an increase in wavelength.

The stability of the CQD was analyzed by recording PL intensity at different experimental parameters like pH, ionic strength, photo irradiation, and time. The PL intensity increases with pH attains a maximum at pH 6 and then decreases (Fig. 6 a). At lower and higher pH values, carbon dot aggregation was likely caused by interactions between surface-bound functional groups with H+ and OH– ions [36]. While the fluorescence intensity remains the same in all concentrations, establishing ionic stability (Fig. 6b). After one hour of UV exposure, PL intensity remained unaltered, confirming their photostability (Fig. 6c). The stability of CQD with time was evaluated by keeping it at room temperature for 3 months and monitoring PL intensity every 15 days. Only a slight change in PL intensity was noted, establishing the stability of the CQD (Fig. 6d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe(II) by CQD through fluorescence method

ce:section.ce:para: To explore the sensing mechanism, the UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence spectra, and fluorescence lifetimes were measured. The excitation spectra of the CQDs and the absorption spectrum of Fe(II) exhibit a good degree of spectral overlap (Fig. S4a). This suggested that the quenching could occur via the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process or the inner filter effect (IFE). However, the lifetime of CQDs in the absence of Fe(II) is measured as 4.83 ns, whereas in the presence of Fe(II), it was measured as 4.75 ns. Here there is not much variation in the lifetime of CQDs after adding Fe(II) ions. This indicates IFE based static quenching mechanism (Fig. S4b) [1]. The sensing mechanism (Scheme 2 ) may be due to photoluminescence quenching and non-radiative relaxation processes. On absorbing sufficient energy by CQDs, electrons get excited, and these electrons then relax back to the ground state with the emission of light in the absence of metal ions. This process is the radiative relaxation process. This gives an emission peak in the PL spectra. The excited electrons of CQDs, on the other hand, interact with metal ions in the presence of metal ions, resulting in a non-radiative relaxation process. As a result, photoluminescence quenching was seen in PL spectra, as evidenced by a steady decrease in peak intensity [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical sensing of Fe(II) by W. Tinctoria carbon dot modified pt electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behavior of the W. tinctoria carbon dot modified electrode was analyzed by doing cyclic voltammetric (CV) studies in the potential range 0 to 1.00 V in a phosphate buffer solution containing 20 µM Fe(II) ion solution at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. Compared to the bare glassy carbon electrode, the created sensor produced a small anodic peak current; however, by adding 0.001 M Fe(II) ion, the current responses are dramatically increased (Fig. 8 a). This confirms the exceptional sensing efficiency of the developed sensor towards Fe(II). To optimize pH value, the effects of buffer pH on the electrochemical response of the developed sensor were examined, pH 7 showed maximum anodic peak and is the optimized pH for Fe(II) ion sensing (Fig. 8b). At a pH of 7, two prominent peaks were detected with the addition of Fe(II) ions. Surface functions on CQDs selectively bond with Fe(II) ions and facilitate electron transfer, resulting in the development of redox peaks. A detailed scan rate study was conducted from 10 to 110 mV/s. The anodic peak current increased proportionally when the scan rate was increased from 10 to 110 mV/s. The increase in current with an increase in scan rate indicates that the voltammetric current is directly proportional to the scan rate (Fig. 8c). The linear graph obtained by plotting square root of scan rate against peak current indicates a diffusion-controlled electrocatalytic process (Fig. 8d) [39].

The selectivity of the developed sensor towards different transition metal ions was analyzed. Fig. 9 a confirms the high selectivity of the developed green sensor towards Fe(II). The created sensor's detection limit and linear range were determined by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The DPV analysis was performed in various Fe(II) concentrations from 5 to 25 nM (Fig. 9b). The calibration curve between Fe(II) concentrations and the peak current showed a straight line plot with a LOD of 2.3 nM (Fig. 9c). A specific electron transfer interaction on the sensor's surface is responsible for the successful detection of Fe(II) ions [40]. The reduction of Fe(II) to Fe results in the reduction peak current, and the oxidation peak current is caused by the oxidation of Fe to Fe(II).

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEIS, photocurrent density, reproducibility, and stability analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The impedance spectra measured at frequencies ranging from 5 Hz to 150 MHz in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and 0.1 M KCl solutions are displayed in Fig. 10 a. The spectrum depicts a semicircle that represents its resistance. The produced sensor exhibits decreased resistance, indicating an increased electron transfer reaction rate. The electroanalytical capabilities of the developed senor towards Fe(II) ion sensing were evaluated by measuring photocurrent density. The intensity of photocurrent increases with the addition of Fe(II) (Fig. 10b). The reproducibility of the sensor was analyzed by comparing the CV runs of the fresh sample and one that was kept in a desiccator for one month. It was surprising to acquire a well-defined peak current with just a slight decline in peak current intensity after one month, demonstrating high reproducibility of the composite (Fig. 10c) [41]. CV analysis for 60 cycles was used to test the stability of the sensor. The peak current of the first and last cycles showed only minor variance, indicating that the developed sensor is relatively stable (Fig. 10d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CQDs probe in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The results of real sample analysis showed that the Fe(II) selective detection recoveries ranged from 99.87 to 100.18 % (Fig. 11 ); these indicate that the sensor could detect Fe(II) ions in the spiked water sample with great accuracy, demonstrating its improved potential for real-time applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation of rhodamine B using ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2

ce:section.ce:para: Several weight percentages of the catalyst are synthesized by the method as described in section 2.3 and were optimized. ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2(15 %) exhibited maximum activity among the different catalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology of ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: XRD patterns were used to analyze the crystallographic features of the as-prepared samples (Fig. 12 a). In the ZrO2 sample, the characteristic peaks at 28.890, 30.970, 43.210, 49.580, and 50.790 were indexed to the (−111), (111), (200), (102), and (220) planes of ZrO2 are compatible with a tetragonal and monoclinic crystal structure (JCPDS Card No. 37–1484 and 42–1164) [42–44]. From Fig. 2a the XRD pattern of CQD shows only a broad peak of about 21.10, attributed to its virtually amorphous properties. In the ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 (15 %) sample, the diffraction peaks located at 24.20, 33.300, and 46.600 correspond to characteristic peaks of monoclinic Bi4O5Br2 (JCPDS No. 00–037-0699) [44]. Due to the low content, there are no related CQD peaks. The morphology of the nanocomposite was investigated using TEM and HRTEM. As seen in the TEM image, the ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 photocatalyst has an irregular sphere-like morphology (Fig. 12b). The fringe spacing of 0.265, 0.321, and 0.273 nm corresponds to the (022) plane of Bi4O5Br2, (110) plane of ZrO2, and (002) plane of carbon dot (Fig. 12c) [45]. The crystalline character of the composite was confirmed by the bright spots in the SAED pattern (Fig. 12d).

According to XPS survey results, the ternary composite consists of Zr, Bi, Br, O, and C elements (Fig. 13 a). Two characteristic peaks at 182.6, and 184.8 eV exhibited in the high-resolution spectra of Zr 3d, may correspond to Zr 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively (Fig. 13b). The high-resolution spectra of Bi 4f exhibited peaks at 159.7 and 165 eV, corresponding to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively (Fig. 13c). A peak at 67.87 eV in the spectra of Br 3d corresponds to Br 3d5/2, which can be attributed to Br- (Fig. 13d). The peaks at 529 and 531 eV in the O 1 s spectrum correspond to the lattice oxygen and hydroxyl group (Fig. 13e) [25]. In the C 1 s spectrum, peaks at 284.8 and 285.7 eV belong to the CC and CO bonds of CQD (Fig. 13f).

The bands around 3000–3400 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum correspond to OH vibrations, the bands at 1642 and 1350 cm−1 correspond to CO and CC bonds, and that in the region 700–500 cm−1 is for the Zr-O-Zr and Bi-O stretching (Fig. 14
a) [25,46]. A diffused reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) examination was performed to predict the activity of the synthesized catalyst. Among the various catalysts, ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 (15 %) exhibits a clear red shift, making the catalyst more susceptible to visible light-mediated photodegradation (Fig. 14 b).

The photocatalytic activity of the developed catalyst was tested with rhodamine B (RhB) dye. The degradation tests were performed in about 12 min, emphasizing the significance of the produced catalyst. The UV–vis. spectra for photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B at 553 nm by CQD, ZrO2, ZrO2-CQD, ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 (15 %) are shown in Fig. 15 . Compared with other photocatalysts, ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 (15 %) shows better activity. The percentage of degradation was calculated using the equation
(1)
D e g r a d a t i o n
( % )
=
[
C 0
-
C t
× 100 ]
/
C 0

Where C0 represents the initial concentration of analyte and Ct represents the concentration of analyte at time t [47]. The bar diagram (Fig. 16 a) depicts a comparison of the degradation efficiency of the different modified composites, ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 (15 %) shows superior efficiency (95.45 %), about 6.6-fold times more than CQD. The kinetics of photodegradation of rhodamine B was studied by plotting –ln C/C0 vs time (Fig. 16b). Kinetics follows pseudo-first-order with a rate constant of 0.218 min−1 for ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 (15 %). The rate constant for ZrO2, ZrO2/CQD, ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 (10 %) and for ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 (20 %) are 0.010, 0.012, 0.216, and 0.172 min−1 respectively. Table S1 is a comparison of the present result with previous reports. Among them, the present catalyst exhibited maximum degradation efficiency in a shorter duration.

Scavenger studies were carried out to explore the effect of reactive species on photocatalytic activity (Fig. 17 ). For this, photodegradation tests were carried out using benzoquinone (BQ), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), and isopropyl alcohol (IPA), as scavengers for .
O
2
-
, h+ and electrons. The results confirm that the degradation rate was restrained significantly by adding BQ than the other compounds. Thus, we can deduce that
. O
2
-
is the major active species in the degradation reaction.

Cyclic voltammetric analysis of the catalyst confirms a higher anodic peak current for the developed catalyst (Fig. 18 a). EIS spectra reveal that ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 (15 %) has the lowest semicircle radius (Fig. 18b), which shows its low resistance power. This indicates its outstanding electrochemical activity among other catalysts. An amperometric study in 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution was used to evaluate the effect of photocurrent on photodegradation. ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 (15 %) shows the highest photocurrent intensity, which indicates the lowest photogenerated carrier recombination rate and best photocatalytic efficiency (Fig. 18c). The charge transfer efficiency of the catalysts was analyzed from the PL spectra. The weakest PL emission intensity for the ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 (15 %) nanocomposite, as exhibited in Fig. 18d. Indicates that the carrier charges are efficiently mobilized and separated. From the above results, we can conclude that the ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 (15 %) sample possesses the lowest recombination for the photoinduced charge carriers, compatible with the outcomes of the photocatalytic experiments.

The recyclability of the developed catalyst was tested by performing photocatalytic degradation for three cycles and analyzing the degradation tendency. An efficient degrading pathway was discovered for three distinct cycles, demonstrating the increased stability of the catalyst (Fig. 19 a). To confirm the degradation of rhodamine B, HPLC analysis was carried out. The mobile phase consists of methanol (65 %) and water (35 %) with a sample injection volume of 25 μL and at a wavelength of 550 nm. A sharp decrease in the intensity of rhodamine B at a retention time of 2.9 min confirms the degradation of rhodamine B (Fig. 19b).

Scheme 3 explains the proposed mechanism of rhodamine B degradation under visible light by ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2(15 %). The band gap of ZrO2 and Bi4O5Br2 are calculated as 4.87 and 2.63 eV from the Tauc’s plot (Fig. 20
a-b), which are close to the results reported in the literature. The Tauc’s plot measurements confirm a lower band gap for ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 (15 %), supporting its higher catalytic activity ( Fig. S5 ). The positive slope of Mott–Schottky plots (Fig. 20c-d) indicates both ZrO2 and Bi4O5Br2 are n-type semiconductors [22,26], and the flat band potentials for ZrO2 and Bi4O5Br2 were obtained as −0.92 and −0.24 V (X-intercept of Mott–Schottky plots). The conduction band (CB) potential was obtained as −0.82 eV for ZrO2 and −0.14 eV for Bi4O5Br2. The valence band potential can be calculated using the relation
(2)
E
V B
=
E
C B
+
E g
Where EVB, ECB, Eg are valence band potential, conduction band potential, and band gap [48] [49]]. EVB for ZrO2 and Bi4O5Br2 are 4.05 and 2.4 eV, respectively.

An n-n heterojunction was created at the interface between ZrO2 and Bi4O5Br2. The band position of ZrO2 travels down to the positive potential, and Bi4O5Br2 moves up to the negative potential to reach Fermi level equilibrium. When the nanocomposite is illuminated by visible light, Bi4O5Br2 absorbs light and produces e-/h+ pairs by exciting electrons from its VB to CB. ZrO2 can be excited to generate electrons and holes by CQDs with an up-conversion characteristic. For the excitation of ZrO2, CQDs transform longer wavelength photons into shorter wavelength lights [26]. The photogenerated electrons of Bi4O5Br2 move to the CB of ZrO2. In the meantime, the photogenerated holes move from VB of ZrO2 to that of Bi4O5Br2. As a result, photoinduced electrons and holes could be effectively separated, and charge carrier recombination was significantly reduced. Thus, the separated charge carriers can take part in the oxidation of rhodamine to produce oxidation products.

In summary, fluorescent carbon dots have been successfully fabricated through a facile and one-pot synthesis route. The optical response of the suggested system was found to be stable in pH variations, saline solution, UV exposure, and over time. Moreover, CQD showed excellent selectivity towards Fe(II) among different analytes. Because of the better electrochemical characteristics of CQDs, electrochemical detection of Fe(II) ions with a LOD of 2.3 nM was possible. We also fabricated a novel ZrO2/CQD/Bi4O5Br2 ternary photocatalyst for visible light-mediated degradation of rhodamine B. The degradation was performed in about 12 min with a degradation efficiency of 95.45 %. Scavenger experiments showed that
O
2
-
is the primary active species in photocatalysis, and based on the findings, a photocatalytic mechanism has been proposed. These findings provide a promising path forward in manufacturing future dual-sensing fluorescent CQDs and ternary photocatalysts that are sustainable, economically viable, and highly stable.

Sneha Mathew: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – original draft. Anu Rose Chacko: Data curation, Visualization. Binila K. Korah: Data curation. Mamatha Susan Punnose: Data curation. Beena Mathew: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Greatly acknowledges the financial assistance received from the University Grants Commission (UGC) in the form of Junior Research Fellowship (JRF).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.154975.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
